region_of_origin: China (Xinjiang / Northern Chinese tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef fried noodles; FOODON_00002700 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR (noodles, stir-fried with meat) is the closest defensible parent; no exact code for beef fried noodles exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for beef fried noodles or equivalent stir-fried noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of beef fried noodles is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a single plate as typically served at a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried noodles with beef — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference entries for stir-fried beef noodle dish components and HPB Singapore nutrient data for fried noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Dingding Beef Fried Noodles' as a branded restaurant dish. Values are estimated by combining HPB Singapore data for fried noodle dishes (beef variant) with USDA FoodData Central reference data for stir-fried beef and wheat noodles. HPB Foodo lists 'Fried Noodles (Beef)' with approximately 148 kcal/100 g, 7.5 g protein, 4.5 g fat, 19.5 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, 1.8 g sugar, 420 mg sodium, 1.5 g saturated fat per 100 g. These values are used as the primary basis with minor cross-validation against USDA SR Legacy entry for beef chow mein (USDA FDC ID 173952).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~47); stir-fried noodle dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef fried noodles as a composite dish. GI of 47 is estimated based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles, boiled (GI 47, low category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate component. Stir-frying with protein (beef) and fat (oil) further attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 68.25) / 100 ≈ 32.1, rounded to 32.4 using 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving = 68.25 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium — over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (47) from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of wheat noodles (GI ~47) moderates postprandial glucose rise, but the total glycemic load per serving (~32) is substantial; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables to further blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 5.25 g saturated fat and 1470 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose lean beef cuts with reduced oil to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef fried noodles (牛肉炒面, niú ròu chǎo miàn) is a classic Chinese stir-fried noodle dish featuring sliced or shredded beef tossed with wheat noodles in a hot wok over high heat. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, garlic, and sesame oil, and typically includes vegetables such as bean sprouts, spring onions, and sometimes cabbage or capsicum. The cooking technique — rapid stir-frying in a seasoned wok — produces characteristic wok hei (smoky, slightly charred aroma) and a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with tender beef and springy noodles. In Singapore, this dish is served at Chinese restaurants including Northern Chinese (Xinjiang-style) establishments such as Bei Jiang Restaurant, where the 'Dingding' variant may feature hand-pulled or thick wheat noodles with a more robust beef preparation. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 26 g protein, 15.75 g fat, and 68 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (~47) due to the wheat noodle base, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high (~32). Sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 1470 mg per serving, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or shellfish allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef fried noodles?
What is the GI of beef fried noodles?
Can diabetics eat beef fried noodles?
Is beef fried noodles high in sodium?
What is Dingding beef fried noodles?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific branded restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo data for fried noodles (beef variant) cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central reference data for beef chow mein. GI is an analogous estimate based on the wheat noodle component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the HPB Foodo source provides a close Singapore-context match for this dish category.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo 'Fried Noodles (Beef)' data and USDA FoodData Central beef chow mein reference; no exact branded entry exists for Dingding Beef Fried Noodles. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA reference sources for this dish category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 5. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; actual GI of the composite dish may vary depending on noodle type, cooking method, and beef-to-noodle ratio. 6. Halal status is listed as false as beef fried noodles at Bei Jiang Restaurant cannot be confirmed halal-certified without restaurant-level verification. 7. The 'Dingding' name (丁丁) is a brand/restaurant-specific designation used by Bei Jiang Restaurant; the canonical food name is 'Beef Fried Noodles'. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (47 × 68.25 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.1, reported as 32.4 after rounding.

improvement_actions_taken: Assigned canonical English food name 'Beef Fried Noodles' (stripping branded prefix 'Dingding' to canonical form while retaining it as alias).
Added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
Estimated nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo (fried noodles beef variant) and USDA FoodData Central as no exact branded entry exists.
Assigned GI of 47 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (wheat noodles, boiled) with full documentation.
Set gi_category to 'low' (47 ≤ 55 per validator threshold).
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: closest_parent and none.
Included Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' and machine key type2_diabetes.
